text:
Feature selection is one of the methods to preprocess high-dimensional data so that machine learning models can achieve the best performance and speed. In this work, we explored a range of methods that can be used for feature selection and we compare two prominent approaches namely SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values and feature importance of a model. Our work utilized the Kaggle Credit Card Fraud Detection Dataset, where we evaluated the performance of five different classifiers such as XGBoost, CatBoost, Random Forest, Extremely Randomized Trees, Decision Trees. A wide variety of SHAP values and model feature importance were obtained by taking different subsets of features from the full dataset and results are compared based on Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), a suitable evaluation metric for imbalanced datasets such as the Credit Card Fraud Detection Dataset.

Experimental results with accompanying statistical analysis (using ANOVA and Tukey’s HSD tests) show that, for most learners and subset sizes, traditional importance-based selection methods lead to better classification performance than SHAP-based selection. SHAP has the advantage of highly interpretable explanations based on game theory, but it is expensive to compute and provides a lower return on investment in terms of model accuracy than the default importance metrics for all learners. We also determine the optimal subset sizes for each learner and obtain the rankings of the different learners. Random Forest and XGBoost with default importance metrics provide the best results. Due to the high cost of SHAP, we find that using the default importance metrics provided by the models leads to better results for larger and higher dimensional datasets, which is common in fraud detection.
